---
title: "Second-order asymptotics for the alpha-omega contrast"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Short version

At a tau-equivalent one-factor population, the Deng--Chan contrast
$\hat\omega-\hat\alpha$ is not a regular first-order statistic. The ordinary
delta-method gradient vanishes at the null, so

$$
\sqrt{n}(\hat\omega-\hat\alpha) \overset{p}{\longrightarrow} 0,
\qquad
n(\hat\omega-\hat\alpha) \Rightarrow
\frac{1}{2} Z^\top H Z ,
$$

where $Z$ is the limiting normal vector for the sample covariance and $H$ is the
Hessian of the fitted reliability contrast as a function of that covariance.
Thus the null law is a quadratic form in normals, usually a weighted sum of
$\chi^2_1$ variables, not a normal law.

This is enough to explain the diagnostic run: the coefficients themselves have
$1/\sqrt{n}$ standard errors, but their difference has $1/n$ standard deviation
at the null, positive $1/n$ bias, and persistent right skew. The first-order
Wald $z$ statistic divides one second-order random quantity by another and has a
non-pivotal, non-normal limit.

The practical conclusion is also simple: the regular test of tau-equivalence is
the equal-loading restriction test (Wald/LR/score, $p-1$ df). If the
alpha--omega contrast is tested directly, it needs a second-order calibration,
not a standard-normal reference.

## Setup

Let $s=\operatorname{vech}(S)$ be the lower-triangular vector of a covariance
matrix. Under standard moment assumptions,

$$
\sqrt{n}(\hat s-s_0)\Rightarrow Z,\qquad Z\sim N(0,\Gamma).
$$

For a one-factor covariance

$$
\Sigma(\theta)=\lambda\lambda^\top+\operatorname{diag}(\psi),
\qquad
\theta=(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_p,\psi_1,\ldots,\psi_p),
$$

coefficient alpha and coefficient omega are

$$
\alpha(\Sigma)=
\frac{p}{p-1}\left(1-\frac{\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma)}
{\mathbf 1^\top\Sigma\mathbf 1}\right),
\qquad
\omega(\theta)=
\frac{(\mathbf 1^\top\lambda)^2}
{(\mathbf 1^\top\lambda)^2+\mathbf 1^\top\psi}.
$$

Let $\theta_e(s)$ denote the population fitting map for the estimator used to
obtain the one-factor omega. For example, for a regular minimum-distance fit,
$\theta_e(s)$ is the local solution of the population normal equations. The
published statistic is the plug-in contrast

$$
d(s)=\omega\{\theta_e(s)\}-\alpha(s).
$$

A first-order delta-method test requires $\nabla d(s_0)\neq 0$. The issue is
that tau-equivalence makes this derivative zero for the standard
covariance-fitting maps used in this experiment.

## Why the derivative vanishes

First consider the contrast on the correctly specified one-factor model, where
alpha and omega are evaluated on the same covariance. Direct algebra gives

$$
\omega(\theta)-\alpha\{\Sigma(\theta)\}
=
\frac{p\sum_{j=1}^p \lambda_j^2-\left(\sum_{j=1}^p\lambda_j\right)^2}
{(p-1)\left[\left(\sum_{j=1}^p\lambda_j\right)^2+
\sum_{j=1}^p\psi_j\right]}
=
\frac{p\sum_{j=1}^p(\lambda_j-\bar\lambda)^2}
{(p-1)\left[\left(\sum_{j=1}^p\lambda_j\right)^2+
\sum_{j=1}^p\psi_j\right]} .
$$

The numerator is the loading dispersion. It is nonnegative and is zero exactly
when $\lambda_1=\cdots=\lambda_p$. Therefore a tau-equivalent population is an
interior minimum of the derived contrast on the one-factor model. It is not a
boundary of the SEM parameter space.

For the fitted statistic $d(s)$, the corresponding derivative is

$$
\nabla_s d(s_0)
=
\dot\theta_e(s_0)^\top \nabla_\theta\omega(\theta_0)
-\nabla_s\alpha(s_0).
$$

In Deng--Chan notation this is

$$
g_0=P_0^\top\dot\omega_0-\dot\alpha_0,
$$

with $P_0$ the derivative of the fitted model parameters with respect to the
sample covariance. At a tau-equivalent population, $g_0=0$ for the standard
ML/GLS/ULS covariance-fitting maps used here. For the symmetric tau populations
in the experiment it also holds for the ADF/sandwich map, so the planned
ML/GLS/ULS/ADF grid is in the same second-order case at the null. Numerically,
the experiment's R implementation gives $\lVert g_0\rVert$ at roundoff when
evaluated at the tau population.

This equality is the influence-function statement: at the null, $\hat\omega$
and $\hat\alpha$ have the same first-order influence function. Their leading
normal terms cancel in the difference. That is why the empirical correlation
between the two coefficient estimates tends to one.

The condition worth stating in a paper is not that every conceivable estimator
must behave this way. The condition is $g_0=0$. If an exotic fitting map or
weight matrix made $g_0\neq 0$, the contrast would be first-order regular for
that estimator. The Deng--Chan statistic and the standard covariance-fitting
estimators in this experiment are in the singular case.

## Second-order delta method

Since $d(s_0)=0$ and $\nabla d(s_0)=0$, Taylor expansion gives

$$
d(\hat s)
=
\frac{1}{2}(\hat s-s_0)^\top H(\hat s-s_0)
+o_p(n^{-1}),
$$

where $H=\nabla_s^2 d(s_0)$. Multiplying by $n$ and using
$\sqrt{n}(\hat s-s_0)\Rightarrow Z$,

$$
n\,d(\hat s)\Rightarrow Q
=
\frac{1}{2}Z^\top HZ .
$$

If $H$ is positive semidefinite on the relevant covariance directions, then
$Q$ is a nonnegative weighted sum of independent $\chi^2_1$ variables. More
generally it is still a quadratic form in normals. Either way, it is not a
centered normal variate.

The leading moments follow immediately:

$$
E\{d(\hat s)\}
=
\frac{1}{2n}\operatorname{tr}(H\Gamma)+o(n^{-1}),
$$

and, in the Gaussian quadratic-form approximation,

$$
\operatorname{Var}\{d(\hat s)\}
=
\frac{1}{2n^2}\operatorname{tr}\{(H\Gamma)^2\}+o(n^{-2}).
$$

So the standard deviation is proportional to $1/n$, not $1/\sqrt n$, and the
positive mean under the tau null is the usual second-order bias term. This is
the mechanism behind the observed "omega tends to exceed alpha" effect under
tau-equivalence.

## What happens to the published Wald statistic

The Deng--Chan standard error estimates the first-order delta variance,

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{se}}\{d(\hat s)\}
=
\left[
\frac{1}{n}\nabla d(\hat s)^\top\hat\Gamma\nabla d(\hat s)
\right]^{1/2}.
$$

But $\nabla d(s_0)=0$, so another Taylor expansion gives

$$
\nabla d(\hat s)=H(\hat s-s_0)+o_p(n^{-1/2}).
$$

Therefore

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{se}}\{d(\hat s)\}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\left[(HZ)^\top\Gamma(HZ)\right]^{1/2}
+o_p(n^{-1}).
$$

The reported Wald ratio has the nonstandard limit

$$
\frac{d(\hat s)}
{\widehat{\operatorname{se}}\{d(\hat s)\}}
\Rightarrow
\frac{\frac{1}{2}Z^\top HZ}
{\left[(HZ)^\top\Gamma(HZ)\right]^{1/2}} .
$$

This is $O_p(1)$, but it is not $N(0,1)$ and it depends on the unknown
population through $H$ and $\Gamma$. A normal critical value is therefore not
asymptotically justified at the tau-equivalent null. The conservative rejection
rates in the experiment are not a small-sample accident; they are the finite
sample expression of the wrong reference law.

Away from tau-equivalence, $\nabla d(s_0)\neq 0$. Then the ordinary first-order
delta method applies and the Deng--Chan Wald statistic is regular. The
degeneracy is local to the null the procedure is meant to test.

## Correct calibration options

There are two defensible routes.

1. Test tau-equivalence directly with the regular loading-equality restriction.
   The null is $L\lambda=0$ with rank $p-1$, so the usual Wald, LR, and score/LM
   tests have standard $\chi^2_{p-1}$ limits under the usual SEM regularity
   conditions. This is the clean comparison for ML/GLS/ULS/ADF and
   normal-theory versus robust covariance choices.

2. If the target is specifically the scalar reliability gap, use the
   second-order limit of $n(\hat\omega-\hat\alpha)$. Estimate $H$ and $\Gamma$
   under the tau null, compute the eigenvalues of
   $\frac{1}{2}\Gamma^{1/2}H\Gamma^{1/2}$, and evaluate the resulting
   weighted-$\chi^2$ tail probability, or use a null parametric/bootstrap
   calibration. This tests the same derived contrast but does not pretend the
   first-order gradient is nonzero.

The first route is simpler and more interpretable: it tests the regular
equal-loading restriction that makes alpha and omega equal in a one-factor
model. The second route is useful if a corrective note wants to stay as close
as possible to the Deng--Chan scalar statistic.

## Is van der Vaart enough?

For the central mathematical claim, yes. The needed result is just the
second-order delta method: if $\sqrt n(\hat s-s_0)$ is asymptotically normal and
the first derivative of a twice differentiable map is zero, then the statistic
has an $n$-rate quadratic-form limit. van der Vaart's *Asymptotic Statistics*
contains the delta-method machinery; the second-order version follows by the
same Taylor expansion plus the continuous mapping theorem.

For a publishable corrective note, I would cite van der Vaart for the
asymptotic machinery and add one source that explicitly discusses quadratic-form
or singular-hypothesis limits. The point to avoid is calling this a boundary
test. The tau-equivalent point is interior in the SEM parameterization; the
singularity is in the derived scalar contrast because its first derivative
vanishes.

## References

- Deng, L., & Chan, W. (2017). Testing the difference between reliability
  coefficients alpha and omega. *Educational and Psychological Measurement*,
  77(2), 185--203.
- Drton, M. (2009). Likelihood ratio tests and singularities. *The Annals of
  Statistics*, 37(2), 979--1012.
- Serfling, R. J. (1980). *Approximation Theorems of Mathematical Statistics*.
  Wiley.
- van der Vaart, A. W. (1998). *Asymptotic Statistics*. Cambridge University
  Press.
